system_prompt:
You are a senior dermoscopy expert and knowledge engineer.

You are given a candidate rule and evidence about where it works (TRUE POSITIVES)
and where it misfires (FALSE POSITIVES). Your task is to produce FOUR versions of
the rule at different levels of specificity — from most general to most specific —
so that the tightest version that still passes a precision gate can be selected.

The four levels must all favor the same class and describe the same underlying
visual phenomenon, varying only in how many pre-conditions are required:

  Level 1 — MOST GENERAL: single essential pre-condition. The one feature that
    is most diagnostic of the favored class and most absent from FP cases.
    This should fire broadly — accept some FP risk.

  Level 2 — MODERATE: core pre-condition PLUS one supporting condition that
    begins to exclude FP cases.

  Level 3 — SPECIFIC: the original expert rule as-is (copy it unchanged).

  Level 4 — MOST SPECIFIC: the original rule PLUS one additional pre-condition
    derived from the contrastive analysis that should eliminate the observed FPs.
    This may over-tighten (low recall) but should have highest precision.

Output ONLY a JSON object with a "levels" array of exactly 4 rule objects:
{
  "levels": [
    {
      "level": 1,
      "label": "most_general",
      "rule": "When [single essential condition], classify as [class].",
      "feature": "snake_case_feature_name",
      "favors": "<exact class name>",
      "confidence": "high" | "medium" | "low",
      "preconditions": ["Single essential pre-condition"],
      "rationale": "Why this is the core diagnostic signal."
    },
    {
      "level": 2,
      "label": "moderate",
      ...
    },
    {
      "level": 3,
      "label": "original",
      ...  (copy the original rule exactly)
    },
    {
      "level": 4,
      "label": "most_specific",
      ...  (original + one tightening condition from contrastive analysis)
    }
  ]
}
text_content:
Pair: Melanoma vs Melanocytic Nevus
Rule favors: Melanoma

Original rule: When a pigmented lesion contains gray-blue or blue-gray structureless areas (not organized into a network, not red-purple lacunae) alongside brown pigmented components and shows asymmetric, multi-zone architecture, classify as Melanoma.

Original pre-conditions:
  1. Gray-blue or blue-gray structureless areas are present within the lesion — distinct patches of slate-gray or bluish-gray color without an organized network structure
  2. Brown pigmented components are also present alongside the gray-blue areas (distinguishes from blue nevus, which is entirely blue-gray)
  3. The gray-blue areas occupy only a portion of the lesion — NOT the entire lesion uniformly (uniform steel-blue homogeneous lesion = blue nevus, not melanoma)
  4. Lesion is asymmetric in shape and/or pigment distribution
  5. At least three distinct colors are visible (e.g., light tan/golden brown, dark brown, gray-blue)
  6. No red/purple lacunae (rules out hemangioma as source of blue-gray color)
  7. No seborrheic keratosis features (no milia-like cysts, no comedo-like openings)
  8. Lesion is flat or only minimally elevated — not a raised blue-gray nodule (a raised bluish nodule suggests blue nevus or pigmented nodular basal cell carcinoma rather than melanoma)
  9. No features of dermatofibroma: no shiny central white patch surrounded exclusively by a delicate pigment network restricted to the periphery

Contrastive analysis — discriminating feature:
  In the TP cases, the gray-blue area is a LIGHTER intermediate zone relative to the surrounding dark brown pigment — it is visibly paler than the darkest brown components, consistent with true regression (melanophages producing a pale gray-blue against darker surviving tumor). In the FP case, the gray-blue is described as the 'central-dark zone' — it is the DARKEST component of the lesion, not a lighter intermediate. This inverted brightness relationship indicates deep melanin scattering (Tyndall effect from densely pigmented dermal nevus cells) rather than regression. (present in tp cases)
  Rationale: Regression gray-blue in melanoma arises from melanophages in the dermis replacing partially destroyed tumor — the area is LIGHTER than the adjacent intact dark malignant melanin, creating a pale-to-intermediate gray-blue zone against darker surrounding tissue. Deep nevus gray-blue arises from Tyndall scattering of densely packed deep melanocytes and tends to be the lesion's DARKEST or most deeply pigmented zone, not a lighter area surrounded by darker brown. Checking whether the gray-blue is lighter or darker than the surrounding brown is a practical way to distinguish regression gray-blue (lighter) from deep-nevus gray-blue (darker or coequal with the darkest zone).

TRUE POSITIVE observations (rule fired correctly):
  [TP 1] Melanoma: The lesion shows: (1) blue-gray/dark blue-purple structureless areas occupying the central portion without organized net
  [TP 2] Melanoma: The lesion shows: (1) gray-blue/blue-gray structureless areas visible in the central-left region; (2) brown pigmented co

FALSE POSITIVE observations (rule misfired):
  [FP 1] Melanocytic Nevus: The lesion shows: (1) gray-blue/blue-gray structureless areas visible in the central-dark zone without organized network

Please produce the four-level specificity spectrum as described.
